abstract: We present \textbf{Deep Researcher Agent}, an open-source framework that enables large language model (LLM) agents to autonomously conduct deep learning experiments around the clock. Unlike existing AI research assistants that focus on paper writing or code generation, our system addresses the full experiment lifecycle: hypothesis formation, code implementation, training execution, result analysis, and iterative refinement. The framework introduces three key innovations: (1) \textbf{Zero-Cost Monitoring} -- a monitoring paradigm that incurs zero LLM API costs during model training by relying solely on process-level checks and log file reads; (2) \textbf{Two-Tier Constant-Size Memory} -- a memory architecture capped at $\sim$5K characters regardless of runtime duration, preventing the unbounded context growth that plagues long-running agents; and (3) \textbf{Minimal-Toolset Leader-Worker Architecture} -- a multi-agent design where each worker agent is equipped with only 3--5 tools, reducing per-call token overhead by up to 73\%. In sustained deployments spanning 30+ days, the framework autonomously completed 500+ experiment cycles across four concurrent research projects, achieving a 52\% improvement over baseline metrics in one project through 200+ automated experiments -- all at an average LLM cost of \$0.08 per 24-hour cycle. Code is available at this https URL.

citations.fullCitation: Chris Lu, Cong Lu, Robert Tjarko Lange, Jakob Foerster, Jeff Clune, and David Ha. The AI scientist: Towards fully automated open-ended scientific discovery. arXiv preprint arXiv:2408.06292, 2024.
citations.justification: This citation is crucial as it represents a key system in the 'AI for science' domain with which the Deep Researcher Agent is directly contrasted. The paper highlights that while AI Scientist can generate full papers, it lacks the core capability for autonomous, iterative GPU experiment execution and result analysis, which is the primary contribution of the Deep Researcher Agent.
citations.title: The AI scientist: Towards fully automated open-ended scientific discovery

citations.fullCitation: John Yang, Carlos E Jimenez, Alexander Wettig, Kilian Lieret, Shunyu Yao, Karthik Narasimhan, and Ofir Press. SWE-agent: Agent-computer interfaces enable automated software engineering. arXiv preprint arXiv:2405.15793, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper is highly relevant as it defines the state-of-the-art for LLM-based agents in the related field of software engineering. The Deep Researcher Agent paper uses SWE-agent as a primary example to differentiate its own focus on long-running, iterative research workflows from the one-shot code generation and bug-fixing tasks targeted by leading coding agents.
citations.title: SWE-agent: Agent-computer interfaces enable automated software engineering

citations.fullCitation: Gaorui Zhang. Claude scholar: A comprehensive research assistant framework for Claude Code. https://github.com/Galaxy-Dawn/claude-scholar, 2026.
citations.justification: This citation is important as it is presented as another contemporary AI research assistant, allowing the authors to clearly define their niche. The paper explicitly distinguishes its own fully autonomous agent from Claude Scholar, which it characterizes as a reactive assistant that helps with writing and knowledge management but does not perform the autonomous experiment execution that is central to the Deep Researcher Agent.
citations.title: Claude scholar: A comprehensive research assistant framework for Claude Code

citations.fullCitation: Takuya Akiba, Shotaro Sano, Toshihiko Yanase, Takeru Ohta, and Masanori Koyama. Optuna: A next-generation hyperparameter optimization framework. In Proceedings of the 25th ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery & Data Mining, 2019.
citations.justification: This citation represents the established field of AutoML and hyperparameter optimization, a traditional approach to automating experiments. The paper includes this to clearly distinguish its agent-based approach, explaining that the Deep Researcher Agent operates at a higher level of abstraction by making qualitative decisions and modifying model architectures, rather than simply optimizing parameters within a predefined search space like Optuna.
citations.title: Optuna: A next-generation hyperparameter optimization framework

intermediateReport:
## Research Paper Report: Deep Researcher Agent: An Autonomous Framework for 24/7 Deep Learning Experimentation with Zero-Cost Monitoring

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The author of the paper is Xiangyue Zhang, affiliated with The University of Tokyo.

### 2. How this work fits into the broader research landscape

The field of deep learning research is characterized by an inherently iterative workflow, involving repeated cycles of experiment design, execution, result analysis, and refinement. This process, often requiring hundreds of repetitions before reaching publishable results, remains largely manual despite its mechanistic components. Recent advancements in Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents have demonstrated capabilities in areas such as code generation, bug fixing, and academic writing. However, existing systems generally do not address the comprehensive, autonomous execution and iterative refinement of GPU-intensive deep learning experiments over extended periods.

For instance, systems like SWE-Agent and OpenHands focus on general software engineering tasks, excelling in one-shot code generation but lacking features for GPU management, training monitoring, or result-driven iteration necessary for deep learning. AI Scientist and Claude Scholar offer functionalities related to research writing and knowledge management, respectively, but do not provide the capability to autonomously launch, monitor, and iterate on deep learning training runs based on observed results.

Traditional AutoML and hyperparameter optimization frameworks, such as Optuna and Ray Tune, facilitate efficient searching within predefined hyperparameter spaces. However, these systems operate at a lower level of abstraction, requiring initial configuration and lacking the ability to make qualitative decisions, modify model architectures, or adapt training pipelines based on holistic analysis of experimental outcomes. Similarly, research agent systems like MLAgentBench and ResearchAgent focus on benchmarks for single-attempt tasks or idea generation from literature, rather than continuous, iterative experimentation and refinement over long durations.

The Deep Researcher Agent positions itself to address this specific gap by providing a framework capable of autonomously managing the full lifecycle of deep learning experiments, from hypothesis formation to iterative refinement, with a focus on cost-efficiency and continuous operation. It aims to bridge the gap between LLM agents skilled in specific tasks and the demands of sustained, data-driven scientific discovery in deep learning.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop an autonomous framework, termed Deep Researcher Agent, that can conduct deep learning experiments continuously, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, with minimal human intervention. The motivation stems from the recognition that the deep learning research workflow, while fundamentally iterative and mechanical in many aspects, is overwhelmingly manual. Researchers are often required to be present to monitor lengthy GPU training runs, interpret results, and decide on subsequent experimental steps, leading to significant time consumption and potential delays in the research process.

A key challenge identified for enabling such autonomy is the prohibitive cost associated with continuous LLM API calls, particularly for monitoring long-running processes like GPU training, which can last hours to days. A naive implementation that polls an LLM frequently for updates would incur substantial costs (e.g., over $50 per day), rendering autonomous 24/7 experimentation economically unfeasible. Furthermore, long-running LLM agents often suffer from unbounded context growth, leading to degraded performance, escalating API costs, and eventual context window overflows.

To overcome these challenges and achieve its overarching goal, the framework sets forth several specific objectives:
1.  **Develop a complete autonomous experiment framework:** This involves designing a system that can manage the entire research lifecycle, including hypothesis generation, code implementation, experiment execution, result analysis, and iterative refinement, structured around a continuous THINK→EXECUTE→REFLECT loop.
2.  **Implement Zero-Cost Monitoring:** The aim is to eliminate LLM API costs during the model training phase, which typically accounts for 90% or more of the wall-clock time in a deep learning experiment cycle. This is critical for economic viability.
3.  **Design a Two-Tier Constant-Size Memory architecture:** The objective is to prevent the issues of unbounded context growth in long-running agents by creating a memory system that maintains a fixed, small size (approximately 5,000 characters) regardless of the agent's operational duration.
4.  **Propose a Minimal-Toolset Leader-Worker Architecture:** This aims to reduce the token overhead associated with LLM API calls by equipping specialized worker agents with only the essential tools required for their specific tasks, thereby optimizing cost and response times.
5.  **Validate through extensive real-world deployment:** The framework's effectiveness, cost-efficiency, and robustness are intended to be demonstrated through continuous, long-term operation across multiple concurrent research projects.

In summary, the motivation is to automate the repetitive and time-consuming aspects of deep learning research to accelerate discovery, while the key objectives are to achieve this autonomously and economically by innovating in monitoring, memory management, and agent architecture.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The Deep Researcher Agent operates as a continuous, autonomous loop structured into three main phases: THINK, EXECUTE, and REFLECT (Algorithm 1, Figure 1). Each cycle begins by taking the project brief and memory log as input to generate an experiment plan. This plan is then executed, monitored to completion, and its results are analyzed before updating the agent’s memory for the next iteration.

**4.1. Zero-Cost Monitoring**
A central component of the design is the Zero-Cost Monitoring paradigm, which addresses the issue of LLM API costs during the typically lengthy GPU training phase. The framework posits that the LLM has minimal utility during active training, as the process follows a predefined schedule and intermediate results are automatically logged. Therefore, during the 90-99% of wall-clock time dedicated to training, the system incurs zero LLM API costs. Instead, it performs lightweight, OS-level checks at configurable intervals (defaulting to 15 minutes):
*   **Process Liveness:** Uses `kill -0 $PID` to verify if the training process is still active.
*   **GPU Utilization:** Employs `nvidia-smi` to confirm active GPU usage, differentiating between a running process and a stalled one.
*   **Log Tail:** Reads the last 50 lines of the training log to capture recent metrics for local logging, without involving the LLM.
The LLM is only re-engaged in the REFLECT phase once the training process has concluded, at which point the collected log data is passed for analysis. This approach reportedly reduces monitoring costs from approximately $0.50 per 8-hour training period to $0.00.

**4.2. Two-Tier Constant-Size Memory**
To counter the problem of unbounded context growth, the framework implements a two-tier memory system designed to remain at a constant size of approximately 5,000 characters (around 1,500 tokens) throughout operation.
*   **Tier 1: Project Brief (B):** This is a human-authored document, frozen at 3,000 characters, outlining the research objectives, codebase structure, constraints, and success criteria. It is unmodifiable by the agent, ensuring research direction stability.
*   **Tier 2: Memory Log (M):** An agent-maintained rolling log, capped at 2,000 characters, comprising two sections:
    *   **Key Results:** Stores milestone experimental outcomes (e.g., "new best" results). This section is automatically compacted, with the oldest entries removed when it exceeds 1,200 characters.
    *   **Recent Decisions:** Logs the agent’s reasoning for each decision. This section is compacted to retain only the 15 most recent entries, regardless of their total character count.
This design ensures a bounded memory footprint, preventing performance degradation and context overflow while mirroring human memory by prioritizing recent decisions and significant outcomes.

**4.3. Leader-Worker Architecture with Minimal Tool Sets**
The system employs a multi-agent Leader-Worker pattern.
*   **Leader Agent:** Functions as the central decision-maker, managing strategic planning and dispatching tasks. It maintains a persistent conversation within each cycle (resetting between cycles) and is equipped with a minimal set of three tools: `log memory`, `write file`, and `read file`.
*   **Worker Agents:** Three specialized agents, each designed with a minimal tool set to reduce token overhead:
    *   **Idea Agent:** For literature search and hypothesis formation (4 tools: `search papers`, `get paper`, `write file`, `read file`).
    *   **Code Agent:** For experiment implementation and execution (5 tools: `run shell`, `launch experiment`, `write file`, `read file`, `list files`).
    *   **Writing Agent:** For generating reports and analysis (3 tools: `write file`, `read file`, `list files`).
Only one worker agent is active at any given time, contributing to cost efficiency. This minimal toolset approach reportedly reduces per-call token overhead by 73% compared to frameworks providing 15+ tools per agent, leading to significant cost savings.

**4.4. Safety Mechanisms**
The framework incorporates several safety features:
*   **Mandatory Dry-Run:** Before launching full GPU training, the Code Agent performs a short dry-run (e.g., 2 forward-backward steps) to verify code execution and catch errors like tensor shape mismatches or missing imports.
*   **Protected Files:** Critical files such as `state.json`, `MEMORY_LOG.md`, and `PROJECT_BRIEF.md` are protected from accidental modification by worker agents.
*   **Human Override:** Provides mechanisms for human intervention via a `HUMAN_DIRECTIVE.md` file, a command-line `--directive` flag, or direct modification of `MEMORY_LOG.md`.
*   **Anti-Burn Protection:** An exponential cooldown mechanism prevents wasteful token consumption during cycles that produce no meaningful output.

**4.5. Deployment Setup**
The framework was evaluated through long-term deployment across four concurrent deep learning research projects. Each project ran an independent agent instance on separate GPU servers equipped with NVIDIA L20X 144GB GPUs. Claude Sonnet with Anthropic’s prompt caching was used as the LLM backbone. Projects spanned diverse deep learning domains.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The evaluation of the Deep Researcher Agent focused on operational metrics, cost efficiency, and performance during sustained, real-world deployment across multiple research projects.

**5.1. Operational Results**
Over 30 days of continuous autonomous operation across four concurrent projects, the framework recorded the following:
*   **Experiment Cycles:** Over 500 autonomous experiment cycles were completed.
*   **Research Progress:** In the project exhibiting the most significant advancement, the agent autonomously explored over 200 configurations, leading to a 52% improvement in the target metric compared to the initial baseline. The improvement trajectory was consistent with diminishing returns, with early experiments yielding substantial gains, followed by more refined optimizations.
*   **Dry-Run Effectiveness:** The mandatory dry-run mechanism proved effective, intercepting 18% of planned experiments before full GPU launch. These pre-training failures were primarily due to issues like tensor shape mismatches or configuration errors. Post-dry-run training crash rates were maintained below 3%.
*   **Human Intervention:** Human directives were observed to be infrequent, approximately once every 3 to 5 days. These interventions were typically for major directional shifts in research (e.g., changing model architecture families), while day-to-day decisions regarding hyperparameter tuning, learning rate schedules, and regularization were handled autonomously by the agent.
*   **Experiment Throughput:** Each project averaged 2–4 experiments per day.

**5.2. Cost Analysis**
A detailed cost analysis demonstrated the economic viability of the framework:
*   **Average LLM Cost:** The average LLM cost was recorded at $0.08 per 24-hour cycle.
*   **Cost Reduction:** This represents a 10–20x cost reduction compared to a conventional LLM polling agent, which was estimated to cost approximately $1.60 per 24-hour cycle. Over a 30-day period, this translates to $2.40–4.80 for the Deep Researcher Agent versus $48.00 for a conventional approach.
*   **Phase-wise Cost:** The cost breakdown showed that the THINK phase incurred approximately $0.05, EXECUTE $0.08, and REFLECT $0.03. Crucially, the MONITOR phase, which constitutes the majority of wall-clock time, incurred $0.00 in LLM costs due to the Zero-Cost Monitoring design.
*   **Cost Control Strategies:** Eight specific strategies contributed to this cost efficiency, including zero-LLM monitoring, constant-size memory, within-cycle prompt persistence, prompt caching, minimal tool sets (achieving a 73% reduction in token overhead), slim prompts, state trimming, and single-worker execution.

**5.3. Memory System Evaluation**
The two-tier constant-size memory system performed as designed:
*   **Steady State:** Tier 2 (Memory Log) reached its steady-state size near its 2,000-character cap within the first week of operation and remained constant thereafter (e.g., 1,978 chars at Day 30), validating the automatic compaction mechanism and bounded memory design. Tier 1 (Project Brief) remained frozen at its fixed size (2,847 characters).

**5.4. Comparison with Existing Frameworks**
The Deep Researcher Agent was positioned as unique among existing AI research tools. While other frameworks excel in specific areas (e.g., Claude Scholar in paper writing, SWE-Agent in general coding), none offered the combination of autonomous experiment execution, zero-cost monitoring, GPU management, 24/7 operation, and constant-size memory that this framework provides.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The Deep Researcher Agent framework presents several significant contributions to the field of AI-driven scientific discovery and the practical application of LLM agents. Its potential impact spans research efficiency, economic viability, and the development of autonomous systems.

Firstly, the framework directly addresses a core bottleneck in deep learning research: the labor-intensive and manual nature of iterative experimentation. By autonomously handling hypothesis formation, code implementation, training execution, result analysis, and iterative refinement 24/7, it could significantly accelerate the pace of scientific discovery. This shifts the role of human researchers from managing repetitive experimental cycles to focusing on higher-level problem definition, creative insights, and interpreting emergent patterns. The demonstrated 52% metric improvement in one project through automated experimentation suggests a concrete impact on research outcomes.

Secondly, the introduction of **Zero-Cost Monitoring** represents a practical innovation that makes continuous LLM-driven experimentation economically viable. Prior to this, the cost of sustained LLM interaction for monitoring long-running processes would be prohibitive. By reducing the average LLM cost to $0.08 per 24-hour cycle, the framework makes 24/7 autonomous research accessible to a broader range of institutions and individual researchers, democratizing access to powerful AI assistance in experimentation. This also provides a model for other long-running LLM agent applications to manage costs effectively.

Thirdly, the **Two-Tier Constant-Size Memory** and **Minimal-Toolset Leader-Worker Architecture** directly tackle fundamental challenges in designing robust and cost-effective long-running LLM agents. The memory system's ability to maintain a bounded context size prevents performance degradation and context overflow, which are common issues in long-duration agent deployments. The minimal toolset approach substantially reduces token overhead, contributing to both cost savings and faster response times, thereby enhancing the overall efficiency of the agent. These architectural patterns offer generalizable solutions for persistent LLM agents across various domains.

Fourthly, the inclusion of **safety mechanisms** like mandatory dry-runs, protected files, and explicit human override channels is critical for practical deployment. These features ensure that the autonomous system operates reliably, mitigates risks of wasted computational resources, and maintains human oversight and control, fostering trust and enabling researchers to intervene when strategic shifts are needed.

Finally, by being released as an open-source framework, Deep Researcher Agent provides a foundational platform for future research and development. It invites the broader research community to build upon its innovations, potentially leading to advancements in areas such as multi-GPU distributed training, more sophisticated metric extraction methods (e.g., using structured logging), and the integration of advanced exploration strategies like Bayesian optimization for more sample-efficient hyperparameter optimization. The work also highlights the ongoing challenge of developing standardized evaluation methodologies for open-ended autonomous research agents, pointing to an important direction for future investigation in AI.
overview:
## Introduction to Autonomous Deep Learning Research

Deep learning research is fundamentally iterative. A typical researcher follows a cycle: forming a hypothesis, writing or modifying code, launching an experiment on a GPU, monitoring progress for hours or days, and then analyzing the results to inform the next step. While parts of this process—such as architecture design—require high-level creative reasoning, the orchestration of these cycles is often mechanical and time-consuming. Researchers often find themselves tethered to their terminals, checking training logs to ensure experiments haven't crashed or diverged.

The Deep Researcher Agent is an autonomous framework designed to automate this entire lifecycle. It aims to operate 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, conducting experiments without human intervention. By integrating Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning with practical system-level execution, the framework manages everything from literature-based hypothesis generation to GPU resource management.

![Deep Researcher Agent Architecture](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05854v1/architecture.png)
*Figure 1: The system architecture of the Deep Researcher Agent, illustrating the THINK-EXECUTE-REFLECT loop, the specialized Leader-Worker model, and the two-tier constant-size memory system.*

The core contribution of this work lies in making autonomous research economically and technically sustainable over long durations. While general-purpose agents exist, they often fail in deep learning contexts due to the high cost of constant monitoring and the "context bloat" that occurs when an agent tries to remember weeks of experimental history. The Deep Researcher Agent introduces specific architectural innovations, such as Zero-Cost Monitoring and Constant-Size Memory, to solve these problems.

## The THINK-EXECUTE-REFLECT Loop

The framework organizes its operations into a continuous loop composed of three primary phases. This structure ensures that every action taken by the agent is grounded in previous results and aligned with the overall research goals.

1.  **THINK**: In this phase, the agent analyzes the current state of the project. It reviews the Project Brief (the researcher's initial instructions) and the Memory Log (the history of previous experiments). Using this information, it identifies the next logical step—be it a hyperparameter tweak, an architectural change, or a new baseline comparison—and formulates a specific plan.
2.  **EXECUTE**: The plan is handed over to a specialized Code Agent. This sub-agent is responsible for modifying the codebase, performing "dry-runs" to catch syntax or tensor-shape errors, and eventually launching the full training process on the GPU.
3.  **REFLECT**: Once the experiment concludes, the agent parses the logs to extract key performance metrics. It compares these results against previous benchmarks to determine if the hypothesis was validated. The Memory Log is then updated with these new insights, and the loop begins again.

This closed-loop system allows the agent to navigate the research space independently, much like a human researcher would during a concentrated period of experimentation.

## Zero-Cost Monitoring: Solving the Economic Barrier

One of the most significant hurdles for autonomous research agents is the cost of monitoring long-running tasks. Deep learning models can take hours or even days to train. A naive LLM agent might poll the system every few minutes, reading the training logs and checking for errors. If an LLM call costs a few cents, frequent polling can easily cost upwards of $\$50$ per day just for observation.

The Deep Researcher Agent introduces a "Zero-Cost Monitoring" paradigm. The framework recognizes that during the active training phase, LLM-level reasoning is rarely needed. Instead of using the LLM, the system employs lightweight, OS-level checks at fixed intervals (e.g., every $15$ minutes).

The monitoring process uses three primary commands:
*   `kill -0 $PID`: This checks if the training process is still running at the operating system level.
*   `nvidia-smi`: This verifies that the GPU is actually being utilized, ensuring the process hasn't stalled or entered a deadlocked state.
*   `tail -n 50`: This captures the latest lines of the log file to ensure metrics are being written.

Because these checks are performed by the local system rather than the LLM, the cost of the monitoring phase $C_{\text{monitor}}$ is exactly $\$0.00$. The LLM is only re-engaged once the process terminates or an error is detected. This approach allows the agent to maintain $24/7$ presence while keeping the total daily cost remarkably low.

## Two-Tier Constant-Size Memory

Long-running agents often suffer from performance degradation as their conversation history grows. This "context bloat" increases API costs and eventually exceeds the LLM's maximum input capacity. The Deep Researcher Agent solves this with a two-tier memory architecture designed to stay at a constant size of approximately $5,000$ characters.

The memory is divided into two distinct components:

### Tier 1: The Project Brief ($B$)
The Project Brief is a static, human-authored document that defines the research boundaries. It contains the primary goals, the location of the codebase, and specific constraints (e.g., "do not modify the data loading logic"). This tier is "frozen" at roughly $3,000$ characters and cannot be changed by the agent, ensuring that the research does not drift away from the original objective.

### Tier 2: The Memory Log ($M$)
The Memory Log is a dynamic document updated by the agent. It stores the history of findings and reasoning. To prevent this log from growing indefinitely, the framework implements an automatic compaction mechanism:
*   **Key Results**: Significant milestones and "best" results are kept. When this section exceeds $1,200$ characters, the oldest entries are deleted to make room for new ones.
*   **Recent Decisions**: This section tracks the logic behind the last $15$ actions. As new decisions are added, the oldest ones are discarded.

By maintaining a total memory size where $\text{Total Memory} = |B| + |M| \approx 5,000 \text{ characters}$, the agent's performance remains stable whether it has been running for one day or $30$ days.

## Efficiency through the Leader-Worker Architecture

To further optimize costs and performance, the framework uses a Leader-Worker model. A central "Leader" agent manages high-level strategy but does not perform technical tasks itself. Instead, it dispatches tasks to specialized "Worker" agents:
*   **Idea Agent**: Specialized in literature search and hypothesis generation.
*   **Code Agent**: Specialized in writing Python code and managing shell commands.
*   **Writing Agent**: Specialized in summarizing results and generating reports.

This specialization is key to reducing "token overhead." In many agent frameworks, every prompt sent to the LLM includes a list of all available tools (e.g., file editors, search engines, compilers). This can add hundreds of unnecessary tokens to every single turn. By giving each worker only the minimal toolset required for its specific job, the Deep Researcher Agent reduces per-call token overhead by $73\%$. This specialization ensures that the Code Agent isn't being "charged" for the search tools it doesn't need to use.

## Reliability and Safety Mechanisms

Autonomous code generation carries risks, particularly when running on expensive GPU hardware. The framework incorporates several safety layers to prevent wasted resources and accidental file corruption.

### The Mandatory Dry-Run
Before starting a full training run that might last $10$ hours, the Code Agent is required to perform a "dry-run." This involves executing the training script for a tiny number of iterations (e.g., $2$ steps). This check catches common failures like:
*   Import errors or missing dependencies.
*   Tensor shape mismatches in a new model layer.
*   Incorrect file paths for datasets.

In experimental trials, this mechanism intercepted roughly $18\%$ of planned experiments before they could waste significant GPU time.

### Human-in-the-Loop
While the agent is autonomous, it is not isolated. The system monitors a specific file called `HUMAN_DIRECTIVE.md`. If a researcher wants to change the project's direction—for example, switching from a Transformer architecture to a Mamba-based one—they can write a note in this file. The agent checks this directive at the start of every THINK phase and incorporates the human feedback into its next plan.

## Empirical Results and Performance

The framework was evaluated over a $30$-day period across four concurrent deep learning projects at the University of Tokyo. The results demonstrated both the efficacy and the economic sustainability of the approach.

Over the course of the month, the agent completed over $500$ experiment cycles. In the most successful project, the agent explored over $200$ different configurations, leading to a $52\%$ improvement in the primary performance metric compared to the initial human-provided baseline. The throughput was consistent, with each project averaging $2-4$ full experiments per day.

The cost analysis was particularly notable. While a standard polling agent was estimated to cost approximately $\$1.60$ per day, the Deep Researcher Agent operated at an average cost of just $\$0.08$ per day. This $10-20\times$ cost reduction makes it feasible to run such agents continuously for months on end.

## Significance of the Work

The Deep Researcher Agent demonstrates that fully autonomous deep learning experimentation is not just a theoretical possibility but a practical and affordable reality. By moving away from the "all-powerful, general-purpose agent" model toward a specialized, cost-conscious architecture, the framework provides a template for long-term AI-driven scientific discovery.

The use of OS-level monitoring and constant-size memory solves two of the most persistent problems in agent design: economic viability and long-term stability. As LLMs continue to improve in their reasoning capabilities, frameworks like this will likely become essential tools in the researcher's kit, handling the mechanical "heavy lifting" of experimentation and allowing humans to focus on the creative frontiers of science.

keyInsights: LLMs offer minimal utility during the active training phase of deep learning experiments; thus, monitoring can be offloaded to local OS-level checks without LLM interaction, significantly reducing costs.

Maintaining a constant-size, two-tier memory system (project brief and rolling memory log) is critical for the long-term stability, performance, and cost-efficiency of autonomous agents by preventing context window overflows.

Employing a leader-worker multi-agent architecture with specialized worker agents, each equipped with only a minimal set of necessary tools, drastically reduces LLM token overhead and improves overall efficiency.
originalProblem: Deep learning research involves a highly iterative, manual workflow of experiment design, execution, analysis, and refinement, leading to significant time consumption and delays.

Existing LLM-based agent systems and traditional AutoML frameworks do not support comprehensive, autonomous execution and iterative refinement of GPU-intensive deep learning experiments over extended periods, nor do they handle continuous monitoring effectively.
The prohibitive cost of continuous LLM API calls and unbounded context growth are major challenges for long-running autonomous deep learning agents.
results: Over 30 days of continuous operation across four projects, the agent completed over 500 autonomous experiment cycles, achieving a 52% improvement in the target metric for one project.

The framework demonstrated an average LLM cost of $0.08 per 24-hour cycle, representing a 10-20x cost reduction compared to conventional LLM polling agents due to zero-cost monitoring and other optimizations.
The two-tier constant-size memory system maintained its bounded size effectively, and safety mechanisms like mandatory dry-runs prevented 18% of potential full GPU training failures.
solution: A Deep Researcher Agent framework autonomously manages the entire research lifecycle, including hypothesis formation, code implementation, experiment execution, result analysis, and iterative refinement, operating 24/7 through a continuous THINK→EXECUTE→REFLECT loop.
Zero-Cost Monitoring is implemented to eliminate LLM API costs during the lengthy model training phase by performing lightweight OS-level checks for process liveness, GPU utilization, and log tails.

A Two-Tier Constant-Size Memory architecture maintains a fixed, small memory footprint (approximately 5,000 characters) to prevent unbounded context growth, while a Minimal-Toolset Leader-Worker Architecture reduces token overhead for specialized tasks.
summary.summary: The Deep Researcher Agent, developed by Xiangyue Zhang at The University of Tokyo, provides an autonomous framework for continuous, 24/7 deep learning experimentation, automating hypothesis generation, code execution, and result analysis. It achieves economic viability through a novel zero-cost monitoring approach and architectural innovations, demonstrating a 10-20x cost reduction and a 52% metric improvement in a target project over 30 days.

title: Deep Researcher Agent: An Autonomous Framework for 24/7 Deep Learning Experimentation with Zero-Cost Monitoring
